myth: Girls with PCOS cannot get pregnant.
fact: Many people with PCOS conceive naturally or with minimal medical support. PCOS affects fertility but does not make pregnancy impossible. Early management greatly improves chances.

myth: You should not exercise during your period.
fact: Light to moderate exercise like walking or yoga during periods can actually reduce cramps and improve mood. Listen to your body and move at your own comfort level.

myth: Irregular periods will automatically become regular after marriage.
fact: Marriage does not change your hormones. Irregular periods are a medical symptom that needs lifestyle attention or medical care, not a life event to fix them.

myth: PCOS only affects overweight girls.
fact: PCOS affects people of all body types. Lean PCOS is a well-recognised condition. Weight is one possible factor, not a requirement for diagnosis.

myth: Touching pickles during periods will spoil them.
fact: There is no scientific basis for this. Menstruation does not change the chemistry of your hands or affect food preservation in any way.

myth: Period blood is dirty or impure.
fact: Menstrual blood is a natural mix of blood, uterine lining, and mucus. It is a sign of a functioning reproductive system, not impurity of any kind.

myth: Eating sour foods during periods makes cramps worse.
fact: There is no scientific evidence linking sour foods to increased cramping. Anti-inflammatory foods like ginger and turmeric are more likely to help than harm.

myth: Facial hair in girls means they are becoming more masculine.
fact: Excess facial hair in people with PCOS is caused by elevated androgens, which are hormones present in all bodies. It has nothing to do with gender identity or masculinity.

myth: If you have PCOS, you must have cysts on your ovaries.
fact: The name is misleading. PCOS is a hormonal condition and not everyone with it has visible cysts. Diagnosis is based on a combination of symptoms, not just an ultrasound.

myth: Girls should not wash their hair during periods.
fact: Washing your hair during periods is completely safe and has no effect on your cycle, flow, or health. Good hygiene during periods is encouraged.

myth: PCOS goes away on its own with age.
fact: PCOS is a long-term hormonal condition that benefits from consistent lifestyle management. While some symptoms may ease after menopause, it does not simply disappear without care.

myth: Acne is just a teenage problem and not related to hormones.
fact: Hormonal acne, especially along the jawline and chin, is very common in PCOS at any age. It is caused by elevated androgens and is a medical symptom, not a hygiene issue.

myth: You cannot enter the kitchen or cook food during your period.
fact: Menstruating people can safely handle food, cook, and be in any part of the home. This restriction has cultural roots but no scientific basis whatsoever.

myth: Eating sugar causes PCOS.
fact: PCOS is caused by a complex mix of genetic and hormonal factors. Excess sugar can worsen insulin resistance in those who already have PCOS, but it does not cause the condition.

myth: Heavy periods mean you are healthier or more fertile.
fact: Very heavy bleeding can actually be a sign of an underlying issue like fibroids or hormonal imbalance. Heavy flow is not a fertility indicator and should be discussed with a doctor.

myth: Girls with PCOS will definitely pass it on to their children.
fact: PCOS has a genetic component but it is not a guaranteed inheritance. Lifestyle factors play a significant role and having a parent with PCOS does not confirm a child will develop it.

myth: Doing yoga or being stress-free will cure PCOS completely.
fact: Yoga and stress management are genuinely helpful for managing PCOS symptoms, but PCOS cannot be cured through lifestyle alone. It is a condition to manage, not eliminate.

myth: Menstruating women should not enter temples or places of worship.
fact: This is a cultural and religious practice in some communities but has no medical basis. Menstruation does not make a person spiritually impure or physically unsafe in any space.

myth: If your periods are regular, you cannot have PCOS.
fact: Some people with PCOS have relatively regular cycles but still experience other symptoms like acne, excess hair growth, or insulin resistance. Regular periods do not rule out PCOS.

myth: Birth control pills cure PCOS permanently.
fact: Hormonal pills can help manage PCOS symptoms while you take them, but they do not treat the underlying condition. Symptoms often return after stopping the medication.

myth: Women with PCOS should avoid all fats in their diet.
fact: Healthy fats found in foods like nuts, seeds, avocado, and ghee actually support hormone health. Eliminating all fat can worsen hormonal imbalance in PCOS.

myth: Emotional sensitivity during periods means you are mentally weak.
fact: Hormonal fluctuations before and during periods genuinely affect mood and emotions. This is a physiological reality, not a character flaw or sign of weakness.

myth: Thin girls do not need to worry about PCOS or hormonal issues.
fact: Hormonal imbalances do not discriminate based on body size. Thin individuals can and do develop PCOS, thyroid issues, and other hormonal conditions.

myth: Having a baby will cure PCOS.
fact: Pregnancy does not cure PCOS. Some symptoms may temporarily change during pregnancy but the underlying hormonal condition remains and requires continued management.

myth: Girls with facial hair due to PCOS should feel ashamed.
fact: Facial hair from PCOS is a medical symptom caused by hormones. It is not a reflection of femininity, attractiveness, or worth. Managing it is a personal choice, not an obligation.

myth: Skipping meals helps with weight-related PCOS symptoms.
fact: Skipping meals spikes cortisol and disrupts insulin levels, which can worsen PCOS symptoms. Regular balanced meals help maintain stable blood sugar and hormone levels.

myth: Only married women need to see a gynaecologist.
fact: A gynaecologist helps people of all ages and marital statuses with hormonal health, period issues, and PCOS management. Marital status has no bearing on healthcare access.

myth: Period pain is normal and you just have to bear it.
fact: Mild discomfort is common but severe pain that disrupts daily life is not something to simply endure. It can indicate conditions like endometriosis and deserves medical attention.

myth: PCOS is a rare condition that very few women have.
fact: PCOS is one of the most common hormonal conditions affecting people with ovaries worldwide. In India, studies suggest it affects roughly 1 in 5 women of reproductive age.

myth: Talking about periods openly is inappropriate or embarrassing.
fact: Open conversations about menstrual health reduce stigma, improve access to care, and help people recognise symptoms earlier. Talking about periods is a sign of awareness, not indecency.
